coredata.dc:title: Uniform zinc deposition on carbon dot modified graphite felt electrode with enhanced electrochemical performance for alkaline zinc-based flow batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yong
Liu, Jia-Xin
Deng, Wei
Li, Hai-Feng
Yu, Guo-Xian
Xiang, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Inhibition of zinc dendrite is one of the important issues for the development of zinc-based flow batteries. In this paper, graphite felts (GFs) are modified by carbon dots (CDs) with various carbon sources. The influence of CDs-modified GFs on zinc deposition, electrochemical properties and battery performance is studied. The results reveal L-ascorbic acid is the best carbon source of CDs to modify GFs, because CDs are well distributed on the surface of GF (GF-VC) to improve the distribution of electric field and then improve the electrochemical performance, while CDs with other carbon sources readily aggerate on the surface of GFs. CDs obtained from L-ascorbic acid can not only replace the carbon defects as new active sites for zinc deposition, but also have the highest content of O-containing functional groups in GFs, which significantly improves their hydrophilicity and the diffusion rate of active substances on the electrode surface. By using GF-VC as anode, the peak power density increases by 18.36 %, and the durability of alkaline zinc-based flow battery (AZFB) can also be significantly improved, while the durability of AZFB by using GFs with CDs obtained from other carbon sources is inferior to GF.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphite felt
Carbon dots
Inhibition of zinc dendrite
Alkaline zinc flow battery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modification of graphite felt with CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22371092), the Excellent Discipline Cultivation Project by Jianghan University (2023XKZ038) and the Scientific Research Project of Jianghan University (2023KJZX02).

This project was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22371092), the Excellent Discipline Cultivation Project by JHUN (2023XKZ038) and the Scientific Research Project of Jianghan University (2023KJZX02).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface morphologies of GF and GF-VC.
IR spectra (a) and Raman spectra (b) of GFs.
XPS general scan spectra and C1s spectra of GFs.
SEM images of zinc deposition at 1 s, 2 s, 30 s, and 180 s time intervals on the surfaces of GF and GF-VC.
HR-TEM of GF-VC (a) and zinc deposition on the GF-VC at 1 s using chronoamperometry (b).
CV (a), LSV (b) and the linear correlation of the oxidation current vs. v 1/2 (c) with GF and CD-modified GF electrodes.
CE (a), VE (b), EE (c) and polarization curves and power density (d) of AZFB with GF and CDs-modified GFs as anodes.

The 1st and 50th charge-discharge profiles of GFs (a), the monopolies (b), XRD pattern (c) and XPS pattern (d) on the surfaces of GF and GF-VC electrodes after the 50th charging-discharging cycle of AZFB.
Element contents of GFs analyzed by XPS general sacn spectra.
The contents of functional groups according to the C1s spectrum.

head.ce:title: Uniform zinc deposition on carbon dot modified graphite felt electrode with enhanced electrochemical performance for alkaline zinc-based flow batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Optoelectronic Materials and Technology, Jianghan University, Wuhan, 430056, HuBei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Optoelectronic Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Optoelectronic Materials and Technology, Jianghan University, Wuhan, 430056, HuBei, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Industrial Fume and Dust Pollution Control, School of Enviroment and Health, Jianghan University , Wuhan, 430056, Hubei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Industrial Fume and Dust Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Industrial Fume and Dust Pollution Control, School of Enviroment and Health, Jianghan University , Wuhan, 430056, Hubei, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inhibition of zinc dendrite is one of the important issues for the development of zinc-based flow batteries. In this paper, graphite felts (GFs) are modified by carbon dots (CDs) with various carbon sources. The influence of CDs-modified GFs on zinc deposition, electrochemical properties and battery performance is studied. The results reveal L-ascorbic acid is the best carbon source of CDs to modify GFs, because CDs are well distributed on the surface of GF (GF-VC) to improve the distribution of electric field and then improve the electrochemical performance, while CDs with other carbon sources readily aggerate on the surface of GFs. CDs obtained from L-ascorbic acid can not only replace the carbon defects as new active sites for zinc deposition, but also have the highest content of O-containing functional groups in GFs, which significantly improves their hydrophilicity and the diffusion rate of active substances on the electrode surface. By using GF-VC as anode, the peak power density increases by 18.36 %, and the durability of alkaline zinc-based flow battery (AZFB) can also be significantly improved, while the durability of AZFB by using GFs with CDs obtained from other carbon sources is inferior to GF.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sustainable energy sources (i.e. wind and solar) can replace fossil energy and effectively alleviate environmental pollution. However, their intermittency and unpredictability pose challenges to the stable supply of electricity [1]. Hence, it is crucial to establish a large-scale and stable energy storage facility that can reliably provide high megawatt-level electricity [2]. Aqueous redox flow batteries (RFBs) have received widespread attention due to their excellent energy and power density, prolonged durability, cost-effectiveness, environmental sustainability, scalability, and adaptable battery design [3]. Among various RFBs, vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) has achieved preliminary commercialization [4]. However, the high cost of vanadium material and low energy density limit its large-scale application [5]. Aqueous zinc-based flow batteries present a convincing case for large-scale energy storage owing to cost-effectiveness, non-toxic nature, high energy density, and recyclability [6]. As compared with the acidic and neutral zinc-based flow battery, alkaline zinc-based flow battery (AZFB) with a higher potential of 1.58 V and higher energy density has attracted wide attention [7].

The practical application of AZFB is significantly influenced by a series of issues associated with zinc anode materials, including the formation of zinc dendrites, zinc corrosion, zinc passivation, hydrogen evolution, and the generation of irreversible by-products [8]. The non-uniform zinc plating/stripping leads to the widespread formation of zinc dendrites during the charging process, resulting in short circuits and shortening battery life [9]. Various strategies have been used to solve the zinc dendrite problem, and the selection of suitable electrode materials is one of the effective strategies [10]. Although the use of carbon felt or graphite felt (GF) to replace traditional zinc electrodes as anode can effectively mitigate the self-corrosion and deformation problems, the problem of zinc dendrite still exists [11]. However, GF electrode lacks the hydrophilicity and electrochemical activity, which can influence the reaction rate and reversibility [12]. These unfavorable factors cannot meet the requirements for practical applications [13]. Moreover, the presence of a "disordered graphite structure" on the surface of GF may lead to electrode polarization, which can further aggravate zinc dendrites [14]. Therefore, the GF electrodes need to be modified properly before use.

High-temperature treatment of GF can not only facilitate a more uniform distribution of zinc deposition on the surface of GFs but also enhance the vacancy on the surface of carbon, which increase the adsoption of electron of zinc [15]. Thus, the design of a new electrode that can suppress the zinc dendrites is one of the main targets to enhance the performance of AZFB [16]. GFs with 3-D porous structure possess a high specific surface area to reduce electrode polarization and internal resistance between the electrode and deposited zinc, which facilitates uniform zinc deposition and effectively retards the formation of zinc dendrites [17]. Related works of metal deposition (such as Bi [18], Pb [19], In Ref. [20], Sn [21], etc.) on the surface of GFs have been reported, where the electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic activity can be enhanced. The deposited metal can also improve the uniform distribution of current density to suppress zinc dendrites. The introduction of heteroatoms such as S [22], N [23], and B [24] on the surface of GF is another crucial strategy to inhibit zinc dendrite. In theory, the introduction of carbon nanomaterials on GF is also a feasible method to improve electrode performance including high specific surface area and excellent chemical stability [25]. CDs represent a prototypical example of 0-D nanocarbon materials, which possess excellent physical and chemical properties, including outstanding electronic conductivity, stable chemical characteristics, large specific surface area, and abundant edge sites. CDs usually contain sp 2/sp 3 hybridized carbon cores and non-crystalline polymer shell with plenty of O/N functional groups [26]. These functionalized surfaces endow CDs with a multitude of distinctive properties, including excellent dispersibility, high conductivity, and readily modifiable electronic structure [27]. Meanwhile, CDs can be tailored to exhibit hydrophilic, lipophilic, or amphiphilic properties as demand, enabling seamless integration with other matrix materials for enhanced physical and chemical properties of matrix materials [28]. For instance, CDs containing polar groups as a functional coating layer can modify the electrolyte/Zn interfaces in zinc ion battery, while those with strong zinc affinity can effectively ameliorate the electric field distribution and ensure that more Zn2+ is adsorbed onto the whole electrode. This is beneficial for lowering the barrier of Zn2+ nucleation and zinc deposition, resulting in a dendrite-free zinc anode [29]. However, the effect of the interaction between GF and CDs on the electrochemical performance of Zn/[Zn(OH)4]2- redox pair remains unclear, and the influence of the physical and chemical structure of CDs-modified GFs on zinc deposition needs to be further clarified.

In this study, CDs-modified GFs were prepared with various carbon sources. The influence of CDs with different physical and chemical properties on the zinc deposition on the surfaces of GFs has been investigated. The interplay of GF and CDs on the electrochemical performance of AZFB has also been studied in detail. The O-containing functional groups (C=O and C-O) were increased obviously in the CDs-modified GFs, which facilitates the diffusion of [Zn(OH)4]2- on the surface of GFs. Meanwhile, the well-distributed CDs obtained form L-ascorbic acid can fill the carbon defect, and act as active sites, which can promote the uniform zinc deposition and inhibit the formation of zinc dendrites, thereby enhance the performance and stability of AZFB.

Zinc oxide (AR), potassium chloride (AR), sodium hydroxide (AR), potassium hydroxide(AR), citric acid (CA, AR),L-ascorbic acid (AC, AR),polyethylene glycol (PEG, CP),glucose (GLU, AR). The chemical reagents were purchased directly from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China without further purification.

2.0 g citric acid, L-ascorbic acid, polyethylene glycol, and glucose were added into deionized water (20 ml) respectively. The samples were then treated by high-temperature microwave for 15 min. The microwave-treated samples were dissolved in deionized water (30 ml), respectively. The pH values were adjusted to 6–7 with NaOH (1 mol L-1). Subsequently, the samples were centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the insoluble solid. The supernatant liquid was poured into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 2000 d and was dialyzed at room temperature for 24 h. In this process, deionized water (200 ml) was renewed every 2 h to remove substances with low molecular weight. The liquid obtained from the dialysis bag was stored at −24 °C for 24 h to achieve complete solidification. Subsequently, the samples were put into a freeze dryer (Free Zone, Labconco, America) at −35 °C for 48 h until the liquid completely disappeared. The solid products were obtained with CDs, named CDs-CA, CDs-VC, CDs-PEG, and CDs-GLU according to the carbon source with citric acid, L-ascorbic acid, polyethylene glycol, and glucose, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of graphite felt with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The solid products obtained from 2.2.1 were dissolved in deionized water (40 ml). The GFs (4 × 4 × 0.42 cm) were added. The GFs were sonicated at room temperature for 30 min, and treated with hydrothermal conditions at 180 °C for 12 h. The GFs were further treated at 80 °C for 2 h in a vacuum drying oven. The CDs-modified GFs prepared by using citric acid, L-ascorbic acid, polyethylene glycol, and glucose as carbon sources are named GF-CA, GF-VC, GF-PEG, and GF-GLU, respectively.

The UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-3300, China) was used to verify the successful synthesis of CDs. The morphology of the samples was characterized by the scanning electron microscope (SU8000, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. The nanostructure of the sample was observed by the transmission electron microscope (FEI Tecnai G2 F20, America). The functional groups on the surface of samples were characterized using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (TENSOR27, Germany). The elemental valence of GFs was analyzed with an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS-SUPRA+, Janpan). The defect structure of the samples was characterized by using a Raman spectrometer (HORIBA HR Evolution, France) under excitation by a 532 nm laser diode.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) were tested with a three-electrode system using an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, Shanghai Chenhua, China). GF or CDs-modified GFs (φ 8 mm), platinum foil electrode, and the Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the working electrode, the counter electrode, and the reference electrode, respectively. The electrolyte for LSV and CV cycle test was composed of 0.05 M [Zn(OH)4]2-, 1.0 M KCl, and 0.8 M KOH, while the electrolyte nucleation overpotential (NOP) test at a scan rate of 40 mV/s was 0.2 M [Zn(OH)4]2-, 1.0 M KCl, and 0.8 M KOH.

The charge-discharge performance of AZFB was conducted by using the LAND battery system (CT3002K, Wuhan LAND Electronics Co., Ltd). The size of the GF electrode was 4 × 4 × 0.42 cm. The membrane was Nafion 212. The catholyte is composed of K4[Fe(CN)6] (0.3 M), KCl (1.0 M), and NaOH (1.0 M) aqueous solution (100 ml), while the anolyte is composed of [Zn(OH)4]2- (0.4 M), KCl (1.0 M), and NaOH (1 M) of aqueous solution (30 ml). During the charge-discharge process, both the catholyte and anolyte were circulated at a certain flow rate. The single battery was operated in a constant current charge-discharge mode, with a charging process of 26 min and a discharging cut-off voltage of 0.5 V. The charging and discharging processes of AZFB were operated with a current density of 80 mA cm- 2.

The UV/vis absorption spectra of quinine sulfate and CDs solution obtained from citric acid, L-ascorbic acid, polyethylene glycol and glucose as carbon sources were shown in Fig. S1, respectively. The UV/vis spectrum of the quinine sulfate displays characteristic band in the range of 200–400 nm. The adsorption bands in the 200–300 nm are attributed to the π-π∗ transition of C=C bond and sp
2 networks in graphene, and those in the 300–400 nm are attributed to the n-π∗ transition of C=O, etc [30]. Compared with quinine sulfate, the UV/vis absorption spectra of CDs derived from different carbon sources show an absorption band in the range of 200–300 nm, while there is an additional band in the range of 300–400 nm for the CD derived from citric acid [31]. The results indicate the successful synthesis of CDs from these carbon sources.

The surface morphologies of GF and CDs-modified GFs are shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. S2, respectively. In GF, the fiber surface is smooth, while there are small particles on the surface of GF-CA (Fig. S2). Larger particles are observed on the surfaces of GF-PEG and GF-GLU. The particle distribution on the surfaces of the GF-CA, GF-PEG, and GF-GLU is not uniform, which is possibly due to the facile aggregation of CDs in the solid state [32]. It is worth noting that a number of extremely fine particles with a more uniform distribution appear on the surface of GF-VC, possibly because L-ascorbic acid is a strong reducing agent, leading to a highly charged peripheral environment around the CDs [33]. The repulsion effect of co-charges may be responsible for the uniform distribution of CDs from L-ascorbic acid. It is well known that the uniform distribution of CDs on the GF surface is beneficial for the distribution of electric field, and then improve the uniform Zn deposition.

The IR spectra (Fig. 2 a) of GFs show the stretching bands at 678, 1073, 1401, 2111, 2970, and 3310 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of -CH-, -C-O, -COO-, -CH-, -CH3/CH2, and -OH groups, respectively [34]. Compared to GF, the stretching intensity of CDs-modified GFs exhibits obvious changes, especially for those of -C-O, -COO-, and -OH groups. The results indicate that the contents of O-containing functional groups increase on the surface of CDs-modified GFs, which may improve their hydrophilicity [35]. It can also enhance the diffusion rate of the active species on the surface of the electrode and reduce the concentration polarization, which is beneficial for uniform zinc deposition during charging process [36]. It is noteworthy that the stretching bands for -OH and -COO- groups for GF-VC electrode are much more intensive than those of GF and the other CDs-modified GFs, indicating that O-containing functional groups on the surface of GF-VC are most abundant. As a result, it possesses the best hydrophilicity, which can facilitate the diffusion of [Zn(OH)4]2-. It also can effectively suppress zinc dendrite on the surface of GF-VC.

The Raman spectra of the GF and CDs-modified GFs are shown in Fig. 2b, which display two types of peaks: the D peak at 1338 cm−1 and the G peak at 1589 cm−1. The former represents the intensity of the disordered carbon, which is caused by the curved graphene and the defect, doping, and edge of the carbon materials. The latter one represents the intensity of ordered carbon and the amount of sp
2 hybridized C atoms. A high ID/IG value reflects great defects present in the carbon structure (ID and IG represent the peak intensity of D and G peaks, respectively) [37]. Fig. 2b illustrates that the ID/IG value of GF is highest, indicating that the GF exhibits the highest abundance of defect, while ID/IG values decrease in CDs-modified GFs. This may be attributed to the formation of graphene structure in CDs-modified GFs. As a result, CDs can effectively reduce material defects and carbon disorder [33]. The ID/IG value of GF-VC is the lowest, indicating the highest graphitization degree, and it is conductive to the promotion of electrical conductivity [38].

The surface element valence for GF and CDs-modified GFs was analyzed by XPS. The XPS general scan spectra of GFs are shown in Fig. 3 , which reveals that the GFs are mainly composed of C and O. The C and O contents of GFs are listed in Table 1
. The O content is only 4.08 % on the surface of GF, while it significantly increases on the surface of CDs-modified GFs. Note that the O content (53.42 %) of CDs-modified GF with L-ascorbic acid as a carbon source is the highest. As shown in Fig. 3, the C1s spectra reveals a shift in binding energies for C-C, C=O, etc. in CDs-modified GFs as compared with that of GF, indicating the strong interaction between CDs and GF. Furthermore, CDs-modified GFs have an additional C(sat) peak at 291.6 eV, corresponding to graphene structure [39]. Thus, these XPS results further validate the successful loading of CDs on the GF surface with a graphene structure (see Table 2).

Fig. S3 depicts the O1s peak spectra of GF and CD-modified GFs, and the content of various functional groups according to the O1s spectra is shown in Table S1. There are mainly three types of oxygen functional groups at 531.3 eV (O=C), 533.0 eV (O-C), and 535.5 eV (H-O-H) in GFs [40]. The highest group content in GF is H-O-H; however, in CD-modified GFs its content decreases while the contents of C=O and C-O groups on the surface significantly increases, which agree with the results of IR spectra.

The zinc deposition behavior on the surface of GF is followed by chronoamperometry at an overpotential of 1 V. The morphologies for GF, GF-CA, GF-PEG, and GF-GLU are shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S4. When zinc deposits on these GFs at 1 s, the fibers of GFs are covered with different sizes of zinc particles. At 2 s, unevenly distributed zinc particles grow and aggregate into larger zinc nuclei. At 30 s, zinc particles begin to grow outward from the nucleus in the form of rod-shaped zinc dendrites. At 180 s, mossy zinc dendrites appear on the surface of the zinc core. As a result, the zinc dendrites on the surface of GF, GF-CA, GF-PEG, and GF-GLU (Fig. S4) have similar loose and sparse mossy patterns, which can significantly affect their electrochemical performance.

In contrast, when zinc is deposited on GF-VC at 1 s, the fibers are uniformly covered by relatively tiny particles; the particles slightly grow at 2 s; zinc particles grow into a uniform tetrahedral structure within 30 s without merging; zinc particles continue growing in a horizontal direction and cover the surface of GF-VC at 180 s. This indicates that the uniform distribution of CDs on the GF-VC surface improves the electric field distribution, thereby suppressing the zinc dendrite during the charging process.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was utilized to study the crystal structure of CDs and the zinc deposition on GF-VC. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the morphology of CDs loaded onto GF fibers shows an interplanar spacing of 0.206 nm, corresponding to that of graphene (100) planes [38]. As shown in Fig. 5b, when zinc is deposited on the surface of GF-VC by using chronoamperometry at 1 s, the interplanar spacing was reduced to 0.182 nm, indicating a pronounced decrease in the strain force generated by the interaction between the zinc atoms and the CDs [41]. The distributed CDs act as zinc nucleation sites to regulate the anchoring of zinc atoms during the charging process. Thus, the uniformly distributed CDs on the surface of the GF-VC electrode can induce uniform zinc deposition.

The nucleation overpotential (NOP) was performed with a CV test at a scan rate of 40 mV/s, and the corresponding curves and data are presented in Fig. 6 a and Table S2. In the CV curve, the potential difference between the initial deposition potential and the cross-over potential is referred to as the zinc NOP [42], which is one of the key parameters of zinc nucleation polarization. A smaller NOP value indicates a lower degree of polarization, leading to more uniform zinc deposition on the surface of GFs and consequently suppressing the formation of zinc dendrite during the charging process. The NOP represents the potential gap that deviates from the equilibrium potential of the reaction on the surface of the electrode, serving as a quantitative measure for electrode depolarization [43]. As shown in Table S2, the results suggest that the NOP of the electrochemical reaction on the surface of GF-VC is decreased as compared to that of GF, and is also smaller than GFs with other CDs obtained from other carbon sources, indicating the lower zinc nucleation polarization. Thus, CDs derived from L-ascorbic acid facilitate mitigating the dendrite formation.

50 continuous CV cycles of various GFs as anode materials were conducted. As shown in Fig. S5. The decreased reduction current may be attributed to the gradual accumulation of zinc dendrites over time [44]. The 50 CV cycles of GF-CA, GF-PEG, and GF-GLU electrodes exhibit similar performance to GF, while 50 CV curves of GF-VC display negligible variation, suggesting superior cyclic stability for the zinc plating/stripping process.

The LSV curves (Fig. 6b) indicate H2 evolution reaction (HER), which has adverse effect for the zinc plating/stripping process. The LSV curve of GF-PEG is essentially identical to that of GF. Compared to GF, the anodic current for GF-VC is more negative, indicating GF-VC is more pronounced to mitigate HER than that on GF and GFs obtained from other carbon sources during the zinc deposition process.

CV tests were also conducted with different scan rates. Fig. 6c shows the plot of the oxidation current as a function of the square root of the scan rate (v 1/2). As shown in Fig. S6, the current density for zinc deposition increases with the increase of the scan rate. The linear relationships between peak current density and v 1/2 indicates that the control process is the diffusion process of [Zn(OH)4]2- on the surface of GFs. Notably, the slope of GF-VC is the steepest, suggesting that the diffusion rate of [Zn(OH)4]2- on the surface of GF-VC is the fastest. It can decrease the concentration polarization on the surface of electrode during charging process.

The energy storage performance is investigated in AZFB with GF or CDs-modified GFs as the anode. The single batteries were carried out at a current density of 80 mA cm- 2 (the flow rate = 120 ml min-1). Fig. 7 shows the coulombic efficiency (CE), voltage efficiency (VE), energy efficiency (EE), and the power density and polarization curves of the single batteries. CE of all single batteries with GF or CD-modified GFs is ∼99 % at the first charging-discharging cycle. However, VE and EE of the single batteries with CDs-modified GFs as anodes exceed that with GF at the first cycle. Discernible variations in the performance stability of a single battery are observed with different GFs as anode materials. As shown in Fig. 7, the VE of all single batteries are stable during 100 cycles. However, the CE of the single battery with GF as the anode is decreased to 87.1 % during 100 cycles, while CE with GF-CA, GF-PEG, or GF-GLU as anode decreased more significantly, possibly due to the shedding of CDs by large size particles on the surface of GFs during charging-discharging cycles. However, CE with GF-VC as anode material exhibits remarkedly enhanced durability of AZFB during 160 charging-discharging cycles. This may be attributed to uniform distributed CDs as the new active site for zinc deposition that suppresses the zinc dendrite, which further results in better durability of AZFB.

The effect of flow rate on the efficiency of single cell with GF and GF-VC as anode are depicted in Fig. S7. VE of single cell with different flow rate has tiny variation. However, the best CE and EE stability of single cell are obtained with the flow rate of 120 ml min-1. The concentration polarization is serious on the surface of GFs in single cell with lower flow rate of 80 ml min-1, while at the high flow rate of 160 ml min-1 it may induce the high pressure of the single cell, which cause the serious transportation of redox-active species across the ion-conducting membrane. The CE, VE and EE during 100 charging-discharging cycles of single cell with GF-VC (Figs. S7a, b, c) are higher than that of single cell with GF (Figs. S7d, e, f) under different flow rate. As shown in Fig. S7, single cells with GF and GF-VC as anode exhibit similar CE (Fig. S7h) across all current densities. Consequently, the EE (Fig. S7j) of both GF and GF-CV as anode has a consistent trend with VE (Fig. S7i).

Fig. 7d shows the power density and polarization curves of AZFB at various current densities (the flow rate = 120 ml min-1). The polarization curves of AZFB with GF and GF-VC as anodes show that the relationship between discharging curves and current densities is nearly linear when current density is below 400 mA/cm2, indicating that there are ohumic polarization regions for AZFB across different current densities. Meanwhile, the discharging potential is inversely proportional to the current densities when current density is below 400 mA cm- 2, indicating that the conversion of active materials on the surface of the GF and GF-VC is governed by diffusion kinetics [45]. However, there are concentration polarization regions for AZFB at higher current densities. The peak power density of AZFB with GF anode reaches 610.30 mW cm−2 at a current density of 480 mA cm−2 while the peak power density with GF-VC anode reaches 722.38 mW cm−2 at a current density of 560 mA cm−2. Compared to AZFB with GF as anode, the peak power density of AZFB with GF-VC as anode has an 18.36 % promotion. These results clearly show GF-VC anode can significantly enhance the performance of AZFB.

Fig. 8ashows the 1st and 50th charging-discharging curves of AZFB. During the 1st charging-discharging process, AZFB with CDs-modified GFs as anodes exhibit a lower charging potential platform, but a higher discharging potential platform as compared to that of GF. The smaller gap between the charging and discharging potential platforms suggests lower ohmic resistance. It is noted that the initial potential of the charging and discharging process significantly increases with GF, GF-CA, GF-PEG, and GF-GLU as anodes, and the gap between the initial potential of the charging and discharging process enlarges at the 50th charging-discharging cycle, which is possibly due to the zinc dendrite accumulation on the surface of GF, GF-CA, GF-PEG, and GF-GLU, leading to the magnification of ohmic resistance of AZFB. As shown in Fig. 8 b and Fig. S7, the large zinc particles are formed on the surface of GF, GF-CA, GF-PEG, and GF-GLU after the 50th charging-discharging cycle. However, the 50th charging-discharging curve of AZFB with GF-VC electrode has negligible change as compared with the 1st charging-discharging curve, indicating the negligible zinc dendrite accumulation on GF-VC surface.

In Fig. 8b, it is clearly shown that fewer zinc particles are formed on the surface of the GF-VC electrode compared to GF after the 50th charging-discharging cycle. Meanwhile, the Zn phase is not observed in the XRD (Fig. 8c) and XPS patterns (Fig. 8d) of GF-VC after the 50th charging-discharging cycle, while it exists in the GF after the 50th charging-discharging cycle. Thus, GF-VC electrode can effectively inhibit zinc dendrite formation, resulting in substantial enhancement of the durability of AZFB, because the uniform CD distribution facilitates to improvement of the electric field distribution.

We have prepared a series of CD-modified GFs by using various carbon sources. These CDs not only increase the O-containing functional groups that could improve the hydrophilicity but also decrease the carbon defects on the surface of GFs. It is noteworthy that CDs obtained from L-ascorbic acid are uniformly distributed on the surface of GF possibly due to the strong reduction preparties of L-ascorbic acid as a carbon source, which effectively improves the electric field distribution on the GF-VC surface, and it can serve as new active site for zinc deposition. However, CDs obtained from other carbon sources readily aggerated on the surface of GF. Both the IR and XPS results validate the O-containing functional groups on the GF-VC surface is most abundant, which facilitates the diffusion of [Zn(OH)4]2-. Thus, CDs obtained from L-ascorbic acid is beneficial for inhibiting zinc dendrite on the surface of GF-VC during the charging process.

When the GF and the CDs-modified GFs have been employed as anode materials, GF-VC could obviously decreases the ΔE (pa-pc) in the CV curve and nucleation overpotential as compared with GF. Moreover, the LSV results reveal that HER is also inhibited effectively on the surface of GF-VC. The diffusion rate of active material on the surface of the GF-VC electrode is also faster than that of GF. Thus, the CDs-modified GF-VC electrode facilitates the zinc uniform deposition, and suppress zinc dendrite during the charging process. In these designed AZFB, the GF-VC electrode exhibits higher performance including power density and durability of charging-discharging cycles than the other electrodes.

Yong Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jia-Xin Liu: Methodology. Wei Deng: Investigation. Hai-Feng Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Guo-Xian Yu: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Jing Xiang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22371092), the Excellent Discipline Cultivation Project by Jianghan University (2023XKZ038) and the Scientific Research Project of Jianghan University (2023KJZX02).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2025.236366.
